context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this state-in-development reflection tbh. idk how to even start bc all the prompt parts blur together for me.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's untangle it together. Before anything else — in your own words, what do you think a 'state' actually is, and what makes it different from, say, a company or an NGO?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the multi-part prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them or scaffolding too heavily, I'll use a Socratic opening question to get them to engage with what they already know. Starting with the most fundamental concept — what the state actually is — will anchor everything else. Keeping it bite-sized and letting them lead with their own thinking.

exchanges.student: uh maybe it’s just like the government? but then idk why that’s diff enough for planning stuff fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Now think about this: a company can also have a lot of power and money. So what can a state do that a company simply cannot? What gives the state a kind of authority that no other actor has?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic intuition that the state is the government, but hasn't yet grasped what makes it distinctive as an actor. Rather than explaining it, I'll push them to think about what unique powers or qualities the state has that other actors don't. This directly addresses one of the prompt's key questions: 'What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?' Using a guiding question to help them discover the concept of legitimacy and coercive authority on their own.

exchanges.student: idk, make laws i guess? but i’m still fuzzy on how that turns into a whole reflection rn.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, making laws is key — now push that further. Why does it matter for planning that the state can make laws? What can it do to other actors — companies, NGOs, citizens — that those actors can't do to each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is onto something important with 'make laws' — this is close to the concept of the state as a rule-maker with legitimate authority. I should affirm the direction without giving it away, then push them to think deeper about why law-making matters specifically for planning. I'll resist the urge to explain the bigger picture of the reflection, and instead keep them focused on building their understanding one step at a time.

exchanges.student: ngl this is where i get stuck, can u just explain the whole state role part kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: That's not how we work here — if I explain it, you lose the chance to actually understand it. And honestly, you're closer than you think. You said the state makes laws. So here's a concrete nudge: can a company force you to pay taxes? Can an NGO put someone in jail? What does that tell you about the state's unique power?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just explain it to them, which goes against the core Socratic and guided discovery rules. I should firmly but kindly decline and redirect them back to the thinking process. They were actually very close to a good insight with 'make laws' — I should point that out and nudge them one more step forward rather than letting them off the hook.

exchanges.student: ok so the state can like enforce stuff on everyone, not just ask. but then the first world vs third world part still scrambles my brain tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — that enforceability over everyone is what makes the state unique. Now, sit with this: do you think all states around the world actually have the same ability to use that power freely? What might make a developing country's state more constrained than, say, the US government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a genuinely important insight — the state's coercive, binding authority over all actors. I should affirm this briefly and then help them build a bridge to the First World vs. Third World distinction using a guiding question. The key idea here is that while all states have this formal authority, not all states have the same capacity or autonomy to use it — especially given external pressures like colonial legacies and globalization. I won't give this away but will prompt them to think about it.

exchanges.student: maybe money stuff? and outside pressure? idk if that’s too vague but i’m confused how deep i’m supposed to go.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — 'outside pressure' is a really important thread. Can you think of a specific example of who or what might pressure a developing country's government? Like, who has leverage over them that they might not have over, say, Germany?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with 'money stuff and outside pressure' — these point toward dependency, conditionality from institutions like the IMF/World Bank, and the influence of globalization. Rather than elaborating, I should validate the direction and push them to be more specific. They're also expressing uncertainty about depth, which I can address briefly without over-scaffolding. I want to keep the momentum going toward their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: maybe IMF or World Bank or like foreign investors? i’m not sure if i’m supposed to talk about all of them or this is turning into too many topics.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, those are great examples — and notice they're not separate topics, they all point to the same idea: that Third World states often have their planning constrained by powerful external actors. Now, how does that connect back to globalization's impact on the state, which the prompt also asks about? Do you see a thread linking these ideas together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified key external actors. I should affirm this and help them see that these examples all point to the same underlying idea rather than being separate topics — which addresses their worry about having too many topics. I also want to start nudging them toward thinking about what this means for their reflection as a whole, since we're getting enough conceptual ground covered that they should start thinking about writing.

exchanges.student: kinda but not really lol, it all feels mashed together rn. can u just tell me the main argument i’m supposed to be making?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the argument — that's yours to make. But look at what you've already figured out in this conversation: the state's unique enforcing power, the constraints on Third World states, the role of external actors like the IMF. Can you try to write one or two sentences connecting those ideas? Just rough, informal — what's the through-line you're seeing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to give them the answer, which I must refuse. However, they're expressing genuine confusion and feeling overwhelmed — the ideas are 'mashed together.' This is a good moment to help them see that 'mashed together' might actually be a feature, not a bug — the prompt's questions are interconnected. Rather than giving them an argument, I should help them articulate what they've already discovered in our conversation so far, and push them to try to write something down.

exchanges.student: maybe like, the state is supposed to lead planning bc it has authority, but in poorer countries that authority gets limited by global actors and markets? i’m still not sure if that’s enough tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid core idea — hold onto it. Now look back at the prompt: it also asks about the state's relationship to industry, the private sector, and social programs. Does your argument say anything about those yet? How might a constrained Third World state handle those relationships differently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong core argument on their own. I should affirm this clearly without over-praising, and push them to think about what's still missing from the prompt — specifically the question about the state's relation to industry, private sector, financial markets, and social programs, which they haven't touched yet. This will help them see their argument has a solid foundation but needs more development. I should also encourage them to start drafting since we've now covered enough ground conceptually.

exchanges.student: idk maybe more pressure to help markets not people? or like privatize stuff? i feel like i need the big picture spelled out tbh.
exchanges.tutor: You actually just laid out the big picture yourself across this whole conversation — you don't need me to repeat it back to you. You have the pieces: state authority, external constraints, market vs. social priorities. The next step is yours: start a rough draft. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the big picture to be spelled out, but they're actually very close to having it themselves. They've now touched on all the major threads of the prompt across our conversation: state authority/legitimacy, First vs. Third World state constraints, external actors, globalization, and now the tension between market-oriented policies and social programs. Rather than spelling it out, I should point out that they already have the big picture and push them to start writing. We've had enough conceptual exchange — it's time to integrate. This is also around exchange 9-10, so I should be steering toward the writing task.
